topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mailman’s delivery seemed too deliberate today, his footsteps echoing unnervingly in the hallway. Every time the elevator chimed, a jolt of ice shot through my chest. I kept glancing at the fire escape, convinced someone was watching from the shadows above. The official notice, tucked into the communal bulletin board, had been posted three days ago, but my eyes kept darting back to it, re-reading the fine print as if the words themselves held some hidden threat. I started locking the deadbolt three times before leaving my unit, checking the lock mechanism with a nervous, repetitive touch. The very air in the apartment felt thick, pressurized, as if something was about to shift, something about to change the layout of my small, familiar space forever.

[story 2]

I spent the afternoon tracing the seams of the drywall in my living room, running my fingers along the edges where the plaster met the baseboard. It felt flimsy, temporary. The whispers started subtly—a faint scraping sound from the ventilation shaft late at night, a muffled thud from the floor above that made my shoulders tense up instantly. I began cataloging everything: the placement of the light fixtures, the exact distance between the doorframe and the wall. Each object seemed positioned with malicious intent, waiting for the moment the structure itself would dissolve. When the realtor finally arrived, his smile seemed stretched too wide, and I just stared at the floor, unable to meet his gaze, certain that his pleasant demeanor was a carefully constructed façade hiding some imminent upheaval.

[story 3]

The sound of the construction crew was an assault. Hammering, the whine of power tools, the incessant, rhythmic banging that vibrated through the soles of my shoes and straight into my bones. I retreated to my bedroom, pulling the heavy curtains shut, trying to build a fortress of darkness. Every vibration sounded like a deliberate dismantling. I kept checking the windows, not for intruders, but for evidence—a misplaced piece of debris, a subtle alteration in the exterior facade. I kept my phone on silent, the screen a cold, unblinking eye in the gloom. When the official letter finally landed on my doorstep, I didn't read it immediately. I just held the paper, my knuckles white, feeling the solid reality of my home begin to feel like shifting sand beneath my feet.
